docs.title: Raspberry Pi Cellular Failover Bridge
docs.text: Turn a Raspberry Pi 4 and a USB LTE modem into a transparent network bridge so your router can fail over to a cellular connection when your primary ISP goes down.

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text: Some routers (like the ASUS BQ16 Pro) support Dual-WAN failover but cannot detect a LTE modem connected via USB. Without a recognized WAN interface, the router has no way to use the cellular connection as a backup.

docs.title: The Solution
docs.text:
Place a Raspberry Pi between the USB modem and the router. The Pi:

  1. Receives an IP from the LTE modem via DHCP.
  2. Performs NAT (IP masquerading) on outbound traffic.
  3. Presents a static IP to the router over Ethernet.

The router sees a normal Ethernet gateway on its secondary WAN port and can handle failover/failback automatically.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Item Details Raspberry Pi Model 4 (or later) with Ethernet port. I used 2Gb model. OS Raspberry Pi OS (Bookworm or later recommended) USB LTE Modem TCL LinkPort or any modem that presents as a USB Ethernet device Ethernet Cable To connect the Pi's built-in Ethernet to the router Router Any router with Dual-WAN / failover support (guide uses ASUS BQ16 Pro) Access SSH or direct terminal access to the Pi

Fresh Image Recommended

  • Starting from a clean Raspberry Pi OS image avoids conflicts with existing network configurations.
  • I used Raspberry Pi OS Lite (64-bit) for Raspberry Pi 4.

docs.title: Guide Overview
docs.text:
This documentation is split into four sections:

  1. Raspberry Pi Configuration — Set up the Pi as a NAT bridge: interface pinning, IP forwarding, iptables rules, and validation.
  2. Router Configuration — Configure your router's Dual-WAN failover to use the Pi as the secondary WAN gateway.
  3. Quick Install (Debian Package) — Automate all Pi configuration with a single package install.
  4. Bridge Monitor — Real-time dashboard and adaptive logging for monitoring bridge activity.

Follow sections 1–2 for manual setup, or use section 3 to automate it. Section 4 adds monitoring and logging on top.

docs.title: Raspberry Pi Configuration
docs.text: A step-by-step guide to turn a Raspberry Pi and a USB LTE modem into a secondary WAN for your router in Failover mode.

docs.title: Topology & Architecture
docs.text:
Network Flow

  • Cellular Network ↔ TCL Linkport (USB) ↔ Pi4 (eth1 = WAN)
  • Pi4 LAN Uplink (eth0 = LAN/Router uplink, 192.168.100.1/24)
  • Routing Logic: Pi acts as a NAT (iptables MASQUERADE) with IP forwarding enabled.
  • Router Logic: Secondary WAN is set to Static IP 192.168.100.10 (Gateway: 192.168.100.1).
graph LR
    LTE((Cellular Network)) <--> Modem[TCL Linkport USB]
    Modem <--> PiWAN[Pi4 eth1: WAN]
    PiWAN <-->|NAT / IP Forwarding| PiLAN[Pi4 eth0: 192.168.100.1]
    PiLAN <--> Router[Router Sec. WAN: 192.168.100.10]
    Router <-->|Failover Logic| Clients[Clients]
    Primary((Primary ISP)) <--> Router

docs.title: Streamlined Package Installation
docs.text:
Install the core networking tools required for the bridge.

Install core networking tools
sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y \
iptables \
iptables-persistent \
netfilter-persistent \
ethtool \
tcpdump \
networkd-dispatcher

Package Explanations

  • iptables: Firewall rules for NAT and security.
  • iptables-persistent: Persist iptables rules across reboots.
  • netfilter-persistent: Persist netfilter rules across reboots.
  • ethtool: View or tune link parameters.
  • tcpdump: Traffic verification and debugging.
  • networkd-dispatcher: Hooks to run link-tuning commands at boot.

docs.title: Interface Pinning & Network Configuration
docs.text: USB modems can sometimes enumerate under new names (e.g., usb0) after a reboot. We lock them down using udev rules, then configure the IPs explicitly.

docs.title: Pin MAC Addresses
docs.text:
1. Run ip link show and note the MAC addresses for your built-in Ethernet and your USB modem.

  2. Create the udev rules:

    Create udev rules
    sudo nano /etc/udev/rules.d/70-persistent-net.rules

  3. Add the following lines to the file:

    /etc/udev/rules.d/70-persistent-net.rules
    SUBSYSTEM=="net", ACTION=="add", DRIVERS=="?*", ATTR{address}=="XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX", NAME="eth0"
SUBSYSTEM=="net", ACTION=="add", DRIVERS=="?*", ATTR{address}=="YY:YY:YY:YY:YY:YY", NAME="eth1"

    Make sure to replace the XX and YY placeholders with your actual MAC addresses!

docs.title: Assign IPs & MTU (NetworkManager)
docs.text:
Create profiles for both interfaces. This assigns a static IP to eth0 and tells eth1 to fetch its IP dynamically from the modem while locking the MTU to 1420 to prevent cellular packet drops.

Configure NetworkManager Profiles
# 1. Set Pi's LAN/Uplink on eth0 (Static)
sudo nmcli connection add type ethernet ifname eth0 con-name "eth0-static" ipv4.addresses 192.168.100.1/24 ipv4.method manual
sudo nmcli connection up "eth0-static"

# 2. Set LTE/WAN on eth1 (DHCP + MTU 1420)
sudo nmcli connection add type ethernet ifname eth1 con-name "eth1-lte" ipv4.method auto 802-3-ethernet.mtu 1420
sudo nmcli connection up "eth1-lte"

NetworkManager Profiles

  • eth0-static: Static IP for the Pi's LAN/Uplink.
  • eth1-lte: Dynamic IP for the LTE/WAN, with MTU locked to 1420.

Clean up any overlapping profiles:

  1. List all profiles:

    List all profiles
    sudo nmcli connection show

  2. Delete any overlapping profiles:

    Clean up default overlapping profiles
    sudo nmcli connection delete "profile to delete"

docs.title: Enable Kernel IPv4 Forwarding
docs.text:
Forwarding allows packets arriving from the router on eth0 to be routed out to the LTE network on eth1 and back.

Enable & persist forwarding
# Enable immediately
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1

# Persist across reboots
echo "net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1" | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/99-ipforward.conf
sudo sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-ipforward.conf

docs.title: Add NAT & Security Hardening (iptables)
docs.text:
This configuration flushes existing rules, rewrites private LAN IPs to the Pi's LTE source IP, adds MSS clamping to fix cellular MTU quirks, and introduces new INPUT drops to secure the Pi from direct external access over the cellular network.

Configure iptables
# Clear existing rules to ensure a clean slate
sudo iptables -F
sudo iptables -t nat -F
sudo iptables -t mangle -F

# NAT: rewrite LAN -> WAN source IPs
sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o eth1 -j MASQUERADE

# Forwarding policy: allow router -> LTE, and return traffic back
sudo iptables -A FORWARD -i eth0 -o eth1 -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A FORWARD -i eth1 -o eth0 -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT

# MSS clamping: adjust TCP MSS to path MTU
sudo iptables -t mangle -A FORWARD -o eth1 -p tcp --tcp-flags SYN,RST SYN -j TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu

# Security Hardening: Drop unsolicited traffic hitting the Pi directly from LTE
sudo iptables -A INPUT -i eth1 -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
sudo iptables -A INPUT -i eth1 -j DROP

# Save rules persistently
sudo netfilter-persistent save
sudo systemctl enable netfilter-persistent

docs.title: Validation & Testing
docs.text:
To test failover:

  1. Reboot both the Pi and your router to ensure a clean state.
  2. Unplug the Primary WAN.
  3. Wait 30–90 seconds and verify your router's Secondary WAN shows as Connected (see Router Configuration for details).

You can monitor the Pi's NAT translation live by running:

Monitor NAT Traffic
sudo watch -n 1 "iptables -t nat -nvL POSTROUTING"

(The packet and byte counters for MASQUERADE should steadily increase as clients browse).

Re-plug the Primary WAN to confirm the router successfully fails back to it after 30–60 seconds.

docs.title: Optimization & Power Saving (Optional)
docs.text:
Since this Pi acts exclusively as a wired bridge, you can disable the onboard Wi-Fi and Bluetooth to reduce power consumption and heat.

  1. Open the boot configuration file: 
    sudo nano /boot/firmware/config.txt
  2. Scroll to the bottom and append these lines: 
    dtoverlay=disable-wifi
dtoverlay=disable-bt
  3. Save, exit, and reboot.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
USB modem not detected

Run the following to check if the system sees the USB device:

bash lsusb dmesg | grep -i usb

If the modem does not appear, try a different USB port or cable. Some modems require a specific mode switch (e.g., usb_modeswitch) to present as a network device instead of a storage device.

eth1 not getting an IP from the modem

Verify the modem is in tethering/hotspot mode, then check:

sudo nmcli connection up "eth1-lte"
ip addr show eth1

If no IP appears, try releasing and renewing DHCP manually:

sudo dhclient -r eth1 && sudo dhclient eth1
NAT counters not incrementing

If MASQUERADE packet counts stay at zero when clients are browsing:

sudo iptables -t nat -nvL POSTROUTING

Verify that IP forwarding is enabled (sysctl net.ipv4.ip_forward should return 1) and that the FORWARD chain rules are present (sudo iptables -L FORWARD -v).

Router health checks failing

Ensure the Pi can reach the internet from the LTE interface:

ping -I eth1 8.8.4.4

If this fails, the issue is between the modem and the cellular network (verify signal, SIM, data plan). If it succeeds, double-check the router's secondary WAN static IP settings match the Pi's eth0 subnet (192.168.100.x).

docs.title: Quick Install (Debian Package)
docs.text: Build and install the raspi-bridge package to automate all Raspberry Pi configuration in one step.

docs.title: Build the Package
docs.text:
On any machine with dpkg-deb available (Raspberry Pi, WSL, or Docker):

Build the .deb
cd raspi-bridge
bash build.sh

This produces raspi-bridge_1.0.0_all.deb in the project root.

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
Copy the .deb to your Raspberry Pi and install:

Install the package
sudo dpkg -i raspi-bridge_1.0.0_all.deb
sudo apt-get install -f   # resolve any missing dependencies

The installer automatically:

  1. Enables IPv4 forwarding (net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1)
  2. Creates NetworkManager profiles (eth0-static, eth1-lte)
  3. Applies iptables NAT + security hardening rules
  4. Reloads udev rules

docs.title: Run the Setup Wizard
docs.text:
After installing, run the interactive setup wizard to pin your interfaces by MAC address:

Configure interface pinning
sudo raspi-bridge-setup

The wizard will:

  • Auto-detect all Ethernet interfaces and their MAC addresses
  • Let you select which is LAN (→ eth0) and WAN (→ eth1)
  • Allow you to override any MAC manually
  • Optionally disable Wi-Fi and Bluetooth for power saving
  • Write the udev rules to /etc/udev/rules.d/70-persistent-net.rules
Re-running the wizard

You can re-run sudo raspi-bridge-setup at any time to change your MAC assignments (e.g., if you swap modems).

docs.title: Verify
docs.text:
After a reboot, confirm everything is working:

Verify configuration
# Check IP forwarding
sysctl net.ipv4.ip_forward   # should return 1

# Check iptables NAT rules
sudo iptables -t nat -nvL POSTROUTING

# Check NetworkManager profiles
nmcli connection show

# Check interface names
ip link show

docs.title: Uninstall
docs.text:
Remove the package
sudo dpkg -r raspi-bridge

This cleanly reverts all configuration changes (removes NM profiles, flushes iptables, disables IP forwarding).

docs.title: Updating
docs.text:
To update raspi-bridge after pulling new changes from the repository:

Update raspi-bridge
# Pull the latest code
cd ~/raspi-cellular-failover-bridge
git pull origin main

# Rebuild the package
cd raspi-bridge
bash build.sh

# Reinstall (automatically re-runs postinst)
cd ..
sudo dpkg -i raspi-bridge_1.0.0_all.deb

docs.title: Docker ARM Testing
docs.text:
You can validate the package on Windows/WSL2 using Docker Desktop with ARM emulation:

Test in ARM container
docker run -it --rm --platform linux/arm64 \
  -v "$(pwd):/mnt" debian:bookworm bash

# Inside the container:
dpkg -i /mnt/raspi-bridge_1.0.0_all.deb
apt-get install -f

docs.title: Bridge Monitor
docs.text: A real-time monitoring tool and adaptive logger for the Raspberry Pi cellular failover bridge.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Live TUI Dashboard — CPU temp, CPU/RAM usage, network I/O per interface, bridge activity detection
  • Adaptive Logging — Polls every 30s for responsive detection; writes every 30 min when idle, every 1 min when active
  • Daily Rotation — One JSON file per day, auto-cleans files older than 30 days
  • Configurable — All intervals and thresholds adjustable via config file

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
Install bridge-monitor
cd bridge-monitor
pip3 install .

docs.title: Live Dashboard
docs.text:
Launch the dashboard
bridge-monitor
# or explicitly:
bridge-monitor dashboard

The dashboard displays:

Metric Description CPU Temperature With color warning at 70°C+ CPU Usage With visual progress bar RAM Usage Used/Total with progress bar eth0 (LAN) I/O RX/TX totals and live rates eth1 (WAN) I/O RX/TX totals and live rates Bridge Status Active (forwarding) or idle Uptime System uptime

Press q to quit.

docs.title: View Configuration
docs.text:
Show current config
bridge-monitor config

docs.title: Manual Logging
docs.text:
Start logging manually
bridge-monitor log

docs.title: Logging Service (Systemd)
docs.text:
For continuous background logging, install the systemd service:

Install the service
cd bridge-monitor
sudo bash install-service.sh

This installs and starts bridge-monitor-logger.service, which runs in the background and writes logs to /var/log/bridge-monitor/.

docs.title: Service Commands
docs.text:
Manage the service
sudo systemctl status bridge-monitor-logger    # Check status
sudo systemctl restart bridge-monitor-logger   # Restart
sudo systemctl stop bridge-monitor-logger      # Stop
sudo journalctl -u bridge-monitor-logger -f    # View live output

docs.title: Updating
docs.text:
To update bridge-monitor after pulling new changes from the repository:

Update bridge-monitor
# Pull the latest code
cd ~/raspi-cellular-failover-bridge
git pull origin main

# Reinstall into the existing virtual environment
cd bridge-monitor
sudo /opt/bridge-monitor/venv/bin/pip install .

# Restart the logging service to pick up changes
sudo systemctl restart bridge-monitor-logger

No need to re-run install-service.sh

The symlink at /usr/local/bin/bridge-monitor already points to the venv binary, so the updated code is available immediately after reinstalling.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Edit /etc/bridge-monitor/config.json:

/etc/bridge-monitor/config.json
{
    "poll_interval_seconds": 30,
    "idle_write_interval_minutes": 30,
    "active_write_interval_minutes": 1,
    "retention_days": 30,
    "bridge_threshold_packets": 10,
    "bridge_window_size": 10,
    "log_directory": "/var/log/bridge-monitor"
}
Setting Default Description poll_interval_seconds 30 How often to poll bridge status (keeps detection responsive) idle_write_interval_minutes 30 How often to write a log entry when bridge is idle active_write_interval_minutes 1 How often to write a log entry when bridge is active retention_days 30 Days to keep log files before cleanup bridge_threshold_packets 10 Minimum average packets/min to consider bridge active bridge_window_size 10 Number of rate samples to average (higher = smoother, slower to react) log_directory /var/log/bridge-monitor Where log files are written

After editing

Restart the service: sudo systemctl restart bridge-monitor-logger

docs.title: Log Format
docs.text:
Logs are stored as JSON Lines (.jsonl) with one file per day:

/var/log/bridge-monitor/
├── bridge_2026-03-01.jsonl
├── bridge_2026-03-02.jsonl
└── bridge_2026-03-03.jsonl

Each line is a JSON object:

Example log entry
{
    "timestamp": "2026-03-03T14:30:00.123456",
    "cpu_temp_c": 48.3,
    "cpu_percent": 12.5,
    "ram_total_mb": 1906.2,
    "ram_used_mb": 342.1,
    "ram_percent": 17.9,
    "uptime_seconds": 86400,
    "bridge_active": true,
    "network": {
        "eth0": {
            "bytes_sent": 123456789,
            "bytes_recv": 987654321,
            "packets_sent": 12345,
            "packets_recv": 98765,
            "send_rate_bps": 1024.5,
            "recv_rate_bps": 4096.2
        },
        "eth1": {
            "bytes_sent": 987654321,
            "bytes_recv": 123456789,
            "packets_sent": 98765,
            "packets_recv": 12345,
            "send_rate_bps": 4096.2,
            "recv_rate_bps": 1024.5
        }
    }
}

docs.title: Bridge Detection
docs.text:
The monitor detects bridge activity by polling the iptables MASQUERADE packet counter:

sudo iptables -t nat -nvL POSTROUTING

docs.title: Sliding Window Algorithm
docs.text:
Instead of a simple on/off check, the detector uses a sliding window average for smooth, self-tuning detection:

  1. Each poll computes the instantaneous packet rate (packets/minute)
  2. The rate is added to a sliding window of the last bridge_window_size samples
  3. The average across the window is compared against bridge_threshold_packets

This naturally handles bursty internet traffic:

  • Heavy traffic fills the window with high values → average stays high → bridge stays active longer
  • Light traffic that stops → zeros push the average down quickly → goes idle sooner
  • Momentary gaps between bursts → some high values remain in the window → no oscillation

A 30-second minimum hold time prevents single-sample flicker at startup.

docs.title: Router Configuration (ASUS BQ16 Pro)
docs.text: This page covers the router-side settings needed to use the Raspberry Pi bridge as a secondary WAN. The examples below use an ASUS BQ16 Pro running firmware 3.0.0.6.102_36998, but the concepts apply to any router with Dual-WAN failover support.

docs.title: Dual WAN Settings
docs.text:
• Enable Dual WAN: ON
  • Primary WAN: Your normal ISP port
  • Secondary WAN: Ethernet LAN (choose port) → cable to Pi eth0
  • Dual WAN Mode: Fail Over
  • Allow failback: OFF (keep disabled during initial testing; enable after the setup is stable — see Failback Guidance below)

docs.title: Auto Network Detection (WAN Health Checks)
docs.text:
These settings control how quickly the router detects a WAN outage and triggers failover.

  • Detect Interval: 5 s
  • Failover / Failback Trigger: 3 fails / 6 passes
  • Network Monitoring: DNS Query + Ping
  • Resolve Hostname: one.one.one.one
  • Ping Target: 8.8.4.4

Why use alternate monitoring IPs?

Using one.one.one.one and 8.8.4.4 instead of 1.1.1.1 and 8.8.8.8 ensures the router's health-check traffic does not get confused with standard client DNS traffic heading to your primary resolvers.

docs.title: Secondary WAN IP Settings
docs.text:
These must match the static IP subnet configured on the Pi's eth0 interface (see Raspberry Pi Configuration).

  • WAN Connection Type: Static IP
  • IP / Mask: 192.168.100.10 / 255.255.255.0
  • Gateway: 192.168.100.1
  • DNS: 1.1.1.1, 8.8.8.8
  • Enable NAT: Yes

docs.title: LAN Port Stability
docs.text:
• Jumbo Frame / EEE / Link Aggregation: Disabled

Energy Efficient Ethernet (EEE)

Disabling these settings prevents link renegotiation and energy-save flaps that cause the secondary WAN to unexpectedly drop.

docs.title: Failback Guidance
docs.text:
Once your failover setup is working reliably:

  1. Run for at least 24–48 hours with Allow failback set to OFF, simulating a few outages to confirm the secondary WAN is stable.
  2. Enable failback by setting Allow failback to ON in the Dual WAN settings.
  3. After enabling failback, unplug the primary WAN, wait for failover, then re-plug it. The router should automatically switch back to the primary WAN within 30–60 seconds (controlled by the failback trigger count × detect interval).

Monitoring failback

Watch the router's WAN status page during the test. If failback does not trigger, verify that the primary WAN link is fully up and that the health-check targets are reachable through it.
